concepts.contentNote: GeeksforGeeks explicitly flags the sibling pitfall P(A | B') Ôëá 1 - P(A | B). The node's misconception list already covers 'wrong conditioning order'; consider adding the more common student error of negating the conditioning event (computing P(E|F') instead of P(E'|F)) as a third misconception, since it is the dual of the one already listed.

sources.verified: By using Bayes' theorem, we have

refreshers.trigger: Student must form the denominator of Bayes' theorem (the total probability of A) by summing weighted likelihoods over every hypothesis, but treats the priors P(Ei) as if they were equal or omits a hypothesis.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: Before we invert anything: if there are several mutually exclusive 'causes' that could each produce the observed event A, how do you compute the overall probability P(A) of seeing A at all?
ladder: Each cause Ei has a known chance of occurring on its own ÔÇö its prior probability P(Ei). Across all causes these priors add to 1.
Given a particular cause Ei, the event A happens with conditional probability P(A|Ei) (the likelihood).
The chance that A happens THROUGH cause Ei is the product P(Ei)┬ĚP(A|Ei).
Since exactly one cause occurs, add these products over every cause to get the total: P(A) = ╬ú P(Ei)P(A|Ei). This sum is exactly the denominator in Bayes' theorem.
refreshers.answer: P(A) = P(E1)P(A|E1) + P(E2)P(A|E2) + ... + P(En)P(A|En). Bayes' theorem then divides the single term P(Ei)P(A|Ei) for the cause you care about by this whole sum: P(Ei|A) = P(Ei)P(A|Ei) / [╬ú_j P(Ej)P(A|Ej)].
refreshers.returnCue: Now that the denominator is just the total probability of A built from priors times likelihoods, apply Bayes' theorem to flip P(A|Ei) into the posterior P(Ei|A).

loadBearingTerms: sample space S and outcomes/elementary events (Class 11 Probability) ÔÇö PRE-CURRICULUM BEDROCK; domain of every definition in the chapter (conditional probability, random variable, partition).
event as a subset of S, and P(E) classical/axiomatic probability for equally likely outcomes (Class 11) ÔÇö PRE-CURRICULUM BEDROCK; assumed throughout, used to compute every P(E), P(F), P(EÔłęF).

intersection EÔłęF (and 'EF' notation), union EÔł¬F, complement E' (Class 11 Sets + Probability) ÔÇö PRE-CURRICULUM BEDROCK; the conditional-probability and complement nodes are built directly on these.
addition rule P(EÔł¬F)=P(E)+P(F)-P(EÔłęF) (Class 11) ÔÇö PRE-CURRICULUM BEDROCK; used in Property 2 of conditional probability and in Example 14.

mutually exclusive (disjoint) events, P(AÔłęB)=0 (Class 11) ÔÇö PRE-CURRICULUM BEDROCK; load-bearing for the independent-vs-mutually-exclusive contrast and for the 'pairwise disjoint' clause of a partition.
exhaustive events / events whose union is S (Class 11) ÔÇö PRE-CURRICULUM BEDROCK; the second defining clause of a partition.
Venn diagram reasoning over S (Class 11 Sets) ÔÇö PRE-CURRICULUM BEDROCK; used to justify Property 2, Example 13 (EÔłęF vs EÔłęF'), and the {EÔłęF', EÔłęF, E'ÔłęF, E'ÔłęF'} partition.

with-replacement vs without-replacement sampling, standard 52-card deck, fair die/coin models (Class 11) ÔÇö PRE-CURRICULUM BEDROCK; the worked examples of the multiplication rule depend on updating counts after a draw.

real-valued function and domain (Class 11 Relations & Functions / Class 12 ch01 Relations and Functions) ÔÇö COVERED UPSTREAM (cbse12 ch01); load-bearing for the random-variable definition. Inline-gloss only.

sum of an infinite geometric progression a/(1-r), |r|<1 (Class 11 Sequences & Series) ÔÇö PRE-CURRICULUM BEDROCK; appears ONLY in Miscellaneous Example 23 (A/B throw a die alternately). Not load-bearing for any of the 9 graded nodes; the NCERT text itself defers it with 'Refer A.1.3 of Class XI Text book'.
bridgesProposed: NONE. Every load-bearing term in this chapter is either pre-curriculum bedrock from Class 11 (basic probability: sample space, event, P(E), Ôłę/Ôł¬/complement, addition rule, mutually exclusive, exhaustive, sampling models) or is taught upstream within cbse12 (real-valued function & domain in ch01 Relations and Functions). There is no idea that is taught NOWHERE upstream, so no AUTHOR-bridge is warranted. The chapter is self-contained for everything genuinely new (conditional probability, multiplication rule, independence, partition, total probability, Bayes).
defers: Infinite geometric series sum a/(1-r) ÔÇö DEFER to Class 11 Sequences & Series (owner). Softening: it surfaces only in Miscellaneous Example 23, which is not one of the nine graded concept nodes; NCERT itself defers it explicitly ('Refer A.1.3 of Class XI Text book'). Do not gate on it.

Mean, variance, and Binomial distribution of a random variable ÔÇö DEFER (out of scope for this node set). The Introduction names them, but the rationalised node graph stops at the bare definition of a random variable; these are owned by the later probability-distribution material and are not load-bearing for any node here.
inlineGlosses: 'real-valued function whose domain is the sample space' (random-variable node): one-line gloss ÔÇö 'a rule that attaches exactly one real number to each outcome in S' ÔÇö sufficient; the function/domain machinery is already owned by cbse12 ch01, so no refresher needed.
'pairwise disjoint' (partition node): one-line gloss ÔÇö 'no two of the pieces share any outcome (every pair has empty intersection)'.
'exhaustive' (partition / total-probability nodes): one-line gloss ÔÇö 'together the pieces use up all of S (their union is S)'.

'prior' vs 'posterior' / 'a posteriori' probability (bayes-theorem node): one-line gloss ÔÇö 'prior P(Ei) = the chance of a cause before seeing the evidence; posterior P(Ei|A) = the updated chance after the event A is observed'. NCERT introduces this terminology in the Remark after the proof; a gloss anchors the otherwise-Latin vocabulary.
